questions.question: What is the difference between omitting the dependency array of a `useEffect` entirely and passing an empty array `[]`?
options: Omitting the array runs the effect after every render; `[]` runs the setup only on mount (and cleanup on unmount).
Omitting the array runs the effect once on mount; `[]` runs it after every render.
Both forms behave identically; the empty array is only a lint convention.
Omitting the array skips the effect entirely; `[]` runs it on every render.
questions.explanation: No deps array means the effect runs after every render; `[]` means it runs setup only once on mount and cleans up on unmount. The other options invert or conflate these behaviors.

questions.question: Which hook construct is the equivalent of the class lifecycle method `componentWillUnmount`?
options: The cleanup function returned from a `useEffect` setup
A `useEffect` with `[]` dependencies
`useState` with a lazy initializer
Computing a value during render instead of storing it
questions.explanation: `componentWillUnmount` maps to the cleanup function `return () => {}` inside `useEffect`, which runs on unmount (and before each re-sync). `useEffect(setup, [])` corresponds to `componentDidMount`, not unmount.

questions.question: When deciding whether a listed dependency has changed between renders, what comparison does React use?
options: `Object.is`
Deep structural equality
The `==` loose-equality operator
JSON serialization equality
questions.explanation: React compares each dependency with `Object.is`. It does not perform deep equality, so a new object/array literal with the same contents is treated as changed.

questions.question: Which styling approach gives locally-scoped, hashed class names with no runtime cost, and is described as the common scoped default?
options: CSS Modules (`*.module.css`)
CSS-in-JS (styled-components / Emotion)
Inline `style` objects
Global CSS (`import "./app.css"`)
questions.explanation: CSS Modules produce locally-scoped hashed class names at build time with no runtime cost, the common scoped default. CSS-in-JS is scoped but adds a runtime; global CSS shares one flat namespace; inline styles are element-only.

questions.question: React ships with a built-in router, data-fetching layer, and global store, so most apps do not need external libraries for these concerns.
questions.explanation: React solves the view, not the app: it has no built-in router, data layer, or global store. You reach for focused libraries (React Router, TanStack Query, Redux Toolkit/Zustand) or a framework like Next.js.

questions.question: When a listed dependency of a `useEffect` changes on an update (re-render), in what order does React run the effect's work?
options: Cleanup of the previous run first, then setup again
Setup again first, then cleanup of the previous run
Only setup; cleanup runs exclusively on unmount
Only cleanup; setup runs exclusively on mount
questions.explanation: On an update where a dependency changed, React runs the previous run's cleanup first, then re-runs setup. Cleanup is not limited to unmount, and setup is not limited to mount.

questions.question: Given this component (StrictMode off), if `roomId` changes from "general" to "random", what happens?
options: The old connection is disconnected (cleanup), then a new connection to "random" is created and connected.
A new connection to "random" is created and connected, but the old one is never disconnected.
Nothing happens because `roomId` is a prop, not state.
The effect runs only its cleanup, leaving no active connection.
questions.explanation: Because `roomId` is in the dependency array, the change triggers a re-sync: cleanup disconnects the old connection, then setup connects to the new room. Effects re-run for changed props, not only state.

questions.question: In the `Cart` example, why is `total = items.reduce(...)` computed during render instead of being stored with `useState`?
options: It is derivable from existing props/state, so storing it would risk the two copies drifting out of sync.
`useState` cannot hold numeric values like a running total.
Reducing the array inside `useState` would cause an infinite render loop.
Derived values must always live in a parent component, never the one that displays them.
questions.explanation: `total` can be computed from `items`, so it is derived, not state; storing derivable data risks the stored copy drifting out of sync with its source. `useState` can hold numbers, and deriving during render does not require a parent.

questions.question: In the Thinking in React method, where should a piece of state live?
options: In the closest common parent of all components that read or write it (or above it).
In the root component, so any component can access it without lifting.
In the leaf component that displays it, to keep rendering local.
In a global store, since local state cannot be shared between siblings.
questions.explanation: Step 4 says to place each piece of state in the closest common parent of all components that read or write it. Defaulting everything to the root or a global store contradicts keeping state as local as possible; leaf placement fails when siblings must share it.

questions.question: How does a child component cause an update to state that lives in its parent?
options: The parent passes a callback prop down; the child invokes it on an event to update state at the owner.
The child mutates the parent's state object directly through a shared reference.
The child calls the parent's `useState` setter, which it imports from the parent module.
The child dispatches a synthetic DOM event that React routes to the parent automatically.
questions.explanation: Step 5, inverse data flow: children can't write parent state directly, so the parent passes callback props (e.g. `onChange`) that the child calls on events to update state at the owner. Children do not mutate parent state or import setters directly.

questions.question: Which statements about React's `StrictMode` are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It double-invokes render, effects, and reducers/state-updaters in development.
Immediately after initial mount it runs each effect as setup → cleanup → setup to simulate a remount.
It is dev-only and has zero behavior in production.
It renders a visible wrapper element around its children.
It permanently double-runs effects in production to harden against missing cleanup.
questions.explanation: StrictMode double-invokes render, effects, and updaters in development, simulates an instant remount (setup→cleanup→setup) after mount, and is dev-only with no production behavior. It renders no UI, and the double-invocation does not happen in production.

questions.question: Which checks does `eslint-plugin-react-hooks` enforce? (Select all that apply.)
options: Only call Hooks at the top level of a component or custom hook.
Only call Hooks from React functions (components or custom hooks).
The `exhaustive-deps` rule flagging missing or stale dependencies in `useEffect`/`useCallback`/`useMemo`.
That every component is wrapped in `React.memo` to prevent re-renders.
That all CSS class names are locally scoped via CSS Modules.
questions.explanation: The plugin enforces the Rules of Hooks (top-level only, from React functions only) plus exhaustive-deps for hook dependency arrays. Wrapping components in `React.memo` and CSS scoping are unrelated to this plugin.

questions.question: When should you pass an explicit type parameter to `useState<T>()` rather than relying on inference?
options: When the initial value is too narrow for the eventual state, e.g. `useState<User | null>(null)`.
Always, because `useState` cannot infer any type from its initial value.
Only when the state holds a primitive such as a `string` or `number`.
Never, because the generic parameter on `useState` is ignored at runtime.
questions.explanation: `useState` usually infers `T` from the initial value; you supply the generic when that value is too narrow, e.g. `useState<User | null>(null)` so the type isn't fixed to `null`. Inference works for primitives, so an explicit type is not required there.

questions.question: Create React App is deprecated, and react.dev now points new projects toward Vite or a framework.
questions.explanation: The reviewer states CRA is deprecated and react.dev now points to Vite (native-ESM dev server, fast HMR, Rollup production build) or a framework.

questions.question: In development with `StrictMode` enabled, a `useEffect` with `[]` deps creates a WebSocket on setup but its cleanup never closes it. What symptom does StrictMode surface that production would hide?
options: After the initial mount the effect runs setup → cleanup → setup, so a leaked second connection (or unbalanced setup) becomes visible immediately.
The component fails to mount at all until the cleanup function is added.
React throws a compile-time error refusing to render the component.
The effect silently runs only once, exactly as it would in production.
questions.explanation: StrictMode simulates an instant remount (setup → cleanup → setup) right after mount, so a missing/incomplete cleanup leaves a leaked connection or unbalanced state that is visible in dev but not in production. It does not block mounting or throw a compile error.

questions.question: The class method `getDerivedStateFromProps` maps to which modern approach, and why is the naive alternative discouraged?
options: Compute the value directly from props during render; mirroring props into state risks the two copies drifting out of sync.
Use a `useEffect` with the prop in its deps to copy it into state, which keeps both copies authoritative.
Store the prop in a `useRef` on every render so the mirrored value persists across renders.
Wrap the component in `React.memo` so React derives the state for you automatically.
questions.explanation: Derive from props directly during render; avoid mirroring props into state, which creates two copies that can drift. Copying a prop into state via an effect is exactly the anti-pattern being avoided, and `React.memo`/`useRef` do not derive state.

questions.question: Which are genuine limitations or costs of using inline `style` objects in React? (Select all that apply.)
options: They cannot express pseudo-classes (`:hover`) or media queries.
A new style object is allocated on every render.
They are best reserved for one-off truly dynamic values.
They are statically scoped at build time with no runtime cost.
Hashed class names prevent collisions across the app.
questions.explanation: Inline styles can't do pseudo-classes/media queries, create a new object each render, and suit one-off dynamic values. Build-time scoping with no runtime cost and hashed collision-free class names describe CSS Modules, not inline styles.

questions.subtopic: ref as a prop in React 19
questions.question: How is passing a `ref` to a function component handled in React 19, and what is the status of `forwardRef`?
options: Function components accept `ref` directly via props; `forwardRef` still works but is slated for deprecation in a future version.
`forwardRef` is mandatory for any function component that receives a `ref`, exactly as before.
`ref` is now passed through the dependency array of `useEffect` instead of as a prop.
Function components can never receive a `ref`; only class components can.
questions.explanation: In React 19 a function component accepts `ref` directly via props with no `forwardRef` needed; `forwardRef` still works but is slated for future deprecation. It is therefore no longer mandatory, and refs are not routed through effect deps.
